_notes: Act 3 — The Drowned Temple. ~100 passages (t_1 through t_91, not all numbers used).
t_1 is the entry from r_70 (Act 2 bridge). The only goto outside t_ space is t_78 -> l_1 (Act 4 bridge).
death_drowned is referenced but not redefined — it already exists in the live adventure JSON.
Three new enemies added: high_priest_morrow_weakened, the_leviathan_aspect_weakened, high_priest_morrow_backstabbed.

Flags set in this act: tide_pressure, had_vision, saw_rear_passage, ritual_delayed, morrow_surprised, morrow_distracted, cleared_corridor, altar_destroyed, morrow_heard, morrow_defeated, knows_the_pact, knows_pact_words, morrow_confronted_with_truth, visited_sanctum, visited_bone_pit, met_drowned_crewmate, knows_morrows_weakness.
The sea_glass_amulet is added unconditionally at t_3 (player may already have it from r_68 — engine handles duplicates silently).
Combat routing for Morrow: t_72 (full), t_22 (weakened/ritual_delayed), t_28 (backstabbed).

t_1.text:
The stairs end. The chanting does not.

You stand at the threshold of something that was never meant to be found. The temple opens before you — not a room but an absence, a space carved from the living rock by hands or forces that understood geometry the way the sea understands patience. The ceiling is lost in darkness above, but the walls glow. Bioluminescent coral crusts every surface in slow, breathing patterns of blue-green light, pulsing with a rhythm that matches the heartbeat you felt on the stairs. The effect is beautiful and deeply wrong, like finding flowers growing on a coffin.

The air is trapped here — a pocket of breath held in the earth's lungs for centuries. It smells of salt and stone and something older than either. Two passages branch from the landing. The left fork ends at a heavy door, barnacle-crusted, with an iron lock green with age. The right fork curves downward toward the source of the chanting — a low, guttural rhythm that vibrates in your teeth and the hollow spaces behind your eyes.

choices.text: Try the locked door on the left
Try the locked door without the key
Follow the chanting to the right
Move quickly — the tide turns in minutes

t_1b.text:
You set the tide chart against the wall and trace the current cycle with your finger. The numbers are unforgiving. High tide reaches this depth in less than an hour. When it does, the air pocket you are breathing will cease to exist.

Everything you do from this point forward is borrowed time.

choices.text: Press on — no time to waste

t_3.text:
The drowned key fits the lock like a finger finding a wound. The mechanism resists, then gives with a sound like cracking bone. The door swings inward on hinges that should have fused with rust centuries ago but move with the oiled ease of something that has been maintained by hands you would rather not think about.

An armoury. That is the only word for it, though the weapons here are not arranged for war but for worship — each one mounted on the wall in careful symmetry, blade-points oriented toward a central pedestal. The coral light gives everything a drowned, spectral quality.

On the weapon rack nearest the door: a cutlass unlike yours. The blade is etched with wave-patterns so fine they seem to move in the shifting light, and when you lift it, it weighs almost nothing. On the pedestal: a shard of green sea-glass on a braided cord, identical to — or perhaps the twin of — the amulets the cultists wear. Beside it, a stoppered clay flask. You pull the cork and the smell hits you. Rum. Good rum. Someone stashed it here a long time ago and the temple's cold has kept it perfect.

You take everything. The door behind you will not lock again.

choices.text: Return to the landing and take the right fork

t_5.text:
The right fork descends in a gradual spiral, the walls narrowing until your shoulders nearly brush the coral on either side. The bioluminescence intensifies as you go deeper — the blue-green glow brightening to something almost like daylight, though no daylight ever had this colour or this patience.

The chanting resolves as you descend. Individual voices now, a dozen or more, weaving a single thread of sound that seems to come from the stone itself. The language is nothing you recognise. The cadence is tidal — rise, hold, fall, hold, rise.

At the bottom of the spiral, the passage opens onto a ledge overlooking an immense space. You stop. You have to stop, because your body refuses to go further until your mind has caught up with what your eyes are seeing.

choices.text: The porthole light follows you down — something watches from behind

t_5b.text:
You feel it before you see it — a pressure at the base of your skull, a sensation like being watched by something that knows your name. You turn.

Nothing is there. But the coral on the wall behind you has changed. Where a moment ago it glowed uniform blue-green, now a pattern has emerged — a circle of brighter light, roughly the size of a ship's porthole. And within it, something that might be a face. Not your first mate's face — something older, something that merely borrowed that shape the way the sea borrows the shape of a shore.

It holds for three heartbeats. You feel grief rise in your throat like saltwater. Then the pattern dissolves back into the general glow and the wall is just a wall again.

But you know, now. Whatever the Leviathan is, it remembers your first mate. It remembers you. The fight on the Windborne was not the end of that conversation.

choices.text: Steady yourself and look out over the cavern

t_10.text:
The Great Cavern.

The name comes to you unbidden, as though the place itself insists on the capital letters. It is vast — a cathedral carved by water over millennia, its ceiling lost in darkness, its floor half-flooded with black water that reflects the coral-light like a second sky. Columns of natural stone rise from the water like the ribs of a drowned leviathan, and between them, on raised platforms of worked stone, kneel the cultists.

Three groups. A dozen in each, arranged in concentric circles around a central altar — a slab of dark stone the size of a ship's wheel, carved with spirals that seem to turn if you look at them too long. Green fire burns on the altar without fuel or smoke, casting shadows that move independently of the light.

And standing at the altar, arms raised, voice carrying above the chanting like a blade above a grindstone — a man. Tall, gaunt, his robes heavier than the others. His face is turned away from you, but you can see the authority in his posture, the way the chanting bends toward him like iron filings toward a lodestone.

Morrow. It must be Morrow.

You are on a high ledge, twenty feet above the cavern floor. Three routes present themselves.

choices.text: Stay and observe — learn the layout before committing
Climb down to the east colonnade — approach through cover
Drop to the cavern floor and head directly for the altar
Survey the full layout from this vantage point

t_12.text:
You press yourself flat against the ledge and watch. Patience is a sailor's virtue — you have spent whole watches reading the sea for signs of weather. This is the same discipline applied to a different kind of storm.

The cavern reveals its secrets slowly. The three groups of cultists are not identical — the inner ring chants in a lower register, the middle ring sways, and the outer ring is still as stone. Rotation. They cycle through the positions, each group moving inward over the course of minutes. A machine made of human bodies.

Behind the altar, partially hidden by a column of natural stone, you spot a narrow passage leading deeper into the rock. A rear entrance to whatever lies beyond the ritual chamber. The cultists ignore it — their attention is entirely on the altar and the green fire.

You also notice the east colonnade — a row of carved pillars along the right wall, creating a covered walkway that runs the length of the cavern. It would provide cover almost all the way to the altar. But something moves in its shadows. A shape that is not a cultist.

t_15.text:
You lower yourself from the ledge and drop to the colonnade floor, landing in a crouch behind the nearest pillar. The carved stone is cold against your back. The chanting masks your movement — a small mercy in a place that offers few.

Then you see it.

The thing in the colonnade is not a cultist. It is something that was a cultist, once, and then became something else — a creature of pale translucent flesh, its body rippling like water held in the shape of a man. The tide wraith drifts between the pillars with a motion that has nothing to do with walking. It leaves a trail of frost on the stone floor.

It has not seen you yet. But the colonnade is narrow, and there is no way past without entering its awareness.

choices.text: Hook across the gap above it
Swing across on the kelp rope

t_15b.text:
The wraith turns. Its face — if you can call it a face — is a hollow impression in translucent flesh, two darker spots where eyes should be, a mouth that opens onto nothing. The cold hits you before it does, a wave of frost that numbs your hands and makes your breath come in ragged clouds.

It moves fast. Faster than something without muscles should be able to move.

t_16.text:
You bring the flintlock up in a smooth motion — aim, breathe, fire. The sound is enormous in the enclosed space, a thunderclap that echoes off the cavern walls and momentarily drowns the chanting. The ball strikes the wraith dead centre.

The creature does not bleed. It shatters. Like a wave breaking on a reef, the translucent body comes apart into a spray of salt water and frost that patters against the stone pillars and is gone. Where the wraith stood, there is nothing but a puddle of brine and a fading chill.

The pistol is spent. You toss the useless weapon aside — no time to reload even if you had powder and shot, which you do not.

t_17.text:
You unsling your rope and judge the distance. A pillar juts from the colonnade wall ten feet above the wraith's patrol line — an anchor point. The throw is awkward in the confined space, but you have thrown lines in gales that would strip paint from a hull. This is nothing.

The hook catches. You haul yourself up and across, hand over hand, your boots dangling three feet above the wraith's translucent head. It pauses. Turns upward. The hollow face considers you with an intelligence that has nothing behind it — no malice, no curiosity, just the patient attention of deep water watching a ship pass overhead.

Then you are past. You drop to the far side of the colonnade and the wraith resumes its drift, as though you were never there. Some things are not worth the effort of killing. Some things simply continue.

t_18.text:
Past the wraith, the colonnade opens onto a wider space — a kind of antechamber at the edge of the ritual circle. From here you can see the altar clearly, no more than forty feet away. The green fire throws Morrow's shadow across the cavern wall behind him — elongated, distorted, the shadow of something that is not entirely the shape of a man.

The chanting surrounds you like a living thing. The cultists' eyes are closed, their faces slack, their mouths moving in perfect unison. They are not aware of you. They are not aware of anything except the rhythm.

You have options. For the first time since you entered this place, you have the advantage of position.

choices.text: Raise the sea-glass amulet toward the chanting circle
Circle behind Morrow through the shadows
Examine a stone carving in an alcove
Try Morrow's sanctum door behind the altar

t_20.text:
You lift the sea-glass amulet and hold it toward the chanting circle. You do not know why. The impulse comes from somewhere below thought — the same place that tells a sailor to reef the sails before the wind changes, before the mind has registered the shift.

The amulet catches the green fire's light and refracts it. For a moment the shard of sea-glass blazes white, and a sound emerges from it — not a sound you hear but a sound you feel, a counter-frequency that slides beneath the chanting like a knife beneath a barnacle. The chanting falters. A cultist in the outer ring opens her eyes and screams — a thin, human sound, the sound of someone waking from a dream they did not choose.

Then the amulet shatters.

It breaks in your hand with a crack like snapping bone. The shards dissolve to salt before they hit the ground. The counter-frequency dies. But the damage is done — the chanting circle has lost its coherence. Cultists stagger, clutching their heads. The green fire on the altar gutters and dims.

Morrow turns.

He is faster than a man his age should be. His eyes find you across the cavern floor with the precision of a hawk sighting prey. The gaunt face contorts — not anger, not surprise, but something closer to recognition. As though he has been expecting you. As though you are late.

t_22.text:
Morrow comes at you and he is diminished. The disrupted ritual has cost him — you can see it in the way his movements stutter, the way his hands tremble as he raises them. The green fire that burns behind his eyes flickers like a candle in a draught. He is still dangerous. But he is no longer inevitable.

'You have no conception,' he hisses, and his voice cracks on the last word, 'of what you are interrupting.'

t_25.text:
You move through shadows that feel almost complicit, as though the darkness of this place has its own opinion about what is happening at the altar and would rather it stopped. The colonnade feeds into a series of alcoves along the cavern's eastern wall — dead priests' crypts, you think, though the occupants have long since been reclaimed by the stone.

From here, you can see Morrow's back. His arms are raised, his voice leading the chanting, his shadow vast and wrong on the wall behind him. He is fifteen feet away, unaware of you. The altar is between you and him — you could reach it in five strides.

A decision crystallises. The kind that cannot be unmade.

choices.text: Strike from behind — no warning, no mercy
Throw something to create a distraction
Fire the pistol into the air as a distraction
Retreat and try a different approach

t_28.text:
You cross the distance in silence. Five strides. Each one feels like a year. On the fourth stride, your boot finds a loose stone and it shifts — the faintest scrape of iron on rock. Morrow's head begins to turn.

You do not give him time to complete the motion.

Your blade catches him across the shoulder, biting deep. The sound he makes is not a scream — it is a gasp, the involuntary exhalation of a man who has been holding his breath for a very long time. He staggers forward, catches himself on the altar, and turns to face you.

Blood on the stone. Blood on your blade. Human blood, red and warm, which surprises you more than it should. Somewhere in the back of your mind you expected something else — something cold, something the colour of deep water. But Morrow bleeds like a man because he is a man. That is the worst part.

His eyes focus on you. The green fire behind them brightens with something that might be fury or might be relief. 'Finally,' he whispers. 'Someone with the courage to—'

He does not finish. His hands come up, wreathed in green light, and the fight begins.

t_29.text: You find a loose chunk of coral on the alcove floor and hurl it across the cavern. It is not a plan so much as an instinct — the same impulse that makes a fox break cover to draw the hounds from its den.

test_luck.lucky_text: The coral strikes a pillar on the far side of the cavern with a sharp crack that echoes like a pistol shot. Morrow's head snaps toward the sound. Three cultists in the outer ring break their chanting to look. For two precious seconds, every eye in the chamber is pointed away from you.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The coral bounces off the altar itself, clattering across the stone surface inches from Morrow's hands. He does not flinch. His head turns slowly — not toward the sound, but directly toward you, as though he has known exactly where you were this entire time. The green eyes find yours across the darkness. 'Come, then,' he says. His voice carries the weight of the ocean.

t_29b.text:
You level the flintlock at the cavern ceiling and fire. The report is deafening — a thunderclap that slams off the walls and ceiling and comes back at you from every direction. Stalactites crack. Cultists scream. The chanting shatters like glass.

Morrow spins toward the sound, hands raised, green fire flaring defensively. He is looking the wrong way. The pistol is spent but it has done its work — you have bought yourself seconds of confusion, and in this place, seconds are worth their weight in lives.

t_29c.text:
You move. The distraction will not last — Morrow is too sharp, too attuned to the rhythms of this place. But for this narrow window of seconds, his back is turned and his attention is elsewhere.

You close the distance to the altar in a dead sprint. Morrow begins to turn. You are faster.

choices.text: Bury your blade in his back

t_30.text:
Subtlety is a luxury you cannot afford. You break from cover and charge.

The cavern floor is slick with brine and scattered with debris — broken coral, the detritus of centuries of tidal wash. You run through it without stopping, without thinking, your weapon in your hand and nothing in your head except the altar and the man who stands before it.

Cultists turn. Mouths open. Hands reach for you. You dodge one, shoulder another aside, feel fingers slide off your coat. The chanting breaks apart around you like a wave around a rock. You are the disruption. You are the thing that should not be here.

Morrow watches you come. He does not move. He does not need to. The green fire on the altar flares higher and the shadows dance and the High Priest of the Drowned stands in the heart of his power and waits for you to reach him with the patient confidence of a man who has the ocean at his back.

'Brave,' he says. His voice is quiet but it carries over everything. 'Brave, and foolish, and far too late.'

t_35.text:
Morrow crosses the cavern floor toward you with the deliberate pace of a man who has nowhere to be and all the time in the world. His robes are heavier than they should be — waterlogged, perhaps, or weighted with something you cannot see. The green fire follows him, trailing from his fingers like phosphorescence in a ship's wake.

Up close, his face is worse than you imagined. Not monstrous — that would be easier. Instead it is the face of an academic. Fine-boned, deeply lined, with the sunken cheeks and hollow eyes of someone who has not slept in months. A man consumed by his work. A scholar who found the wrong answer.

The cultists have stopped chanting. The cavern is silent except for the sound of water lapping against stone. Morrow stops ten feet from you and tilts his head, examining you the way a naturalist examines a specimen.

'The amulet,' he says. His voice is precise, clipped. 'Where did you find it? The resonance frequency was exact. That is not a common artifact.' He pauses. Something flickers behind the green fire in his eyes — curiosity, pure and terrible. 'Who are you?'

choices.text: Face him now — he is weakened

t_38.text:
The sanctum door is set into the cavern wall behind the altar, half-hidden by a curtain of living kelp. The lock is the same green-corroded iron as the armoury door — the same hand made both. The same key, perhaps.

Beyond the door: silence. A different quality of dark. The air smells of ink and old paper and the particular despair of a man who has been alone with his thoughts for far too long.

choices.text: The door is locked — try another way

t_40.text:
Morrow's sanctum.

The room is smaller than you expected — the private space of a man who has compressed his entire world into a ten-foot square. Bookshelves line three walls, crammed with waterproofed volumes and scrolls in languages you do not recognise. A writing desk faces the fourth wall, buried under papers. Charts of the Sunken Reaches pinned to every available surface, annotated in a hand that starts precise and degrades into something barely legible.

A locked chest sits beneath the desk. The journal on the writing surface is still open, the ink not yet dry on the final entry. The man was writing when the ritual began. He was still trying to understand what he was doing even as he did it.

The room smells of lamp oil and desperation.

choices.text: Check the supply alcove behind the sanctum
Leave — there is no time for this

t_42.text:
You pick up the journal. It is bound in oilskin, waterproofed with the same care a sailor would give a ship's log. The pages are dense with writing — months of entries, maybe years. The early pages are careful, academic, the handwriting of a man who annotates his own annotations. The later pages are a different matter.

The handwriting degrades as you turn the pages. Not from haste — from something else. The letters elongate, the loops tighten, the words crowd together as though fighting for space on the page. By the final third, the writing is almost illegible, the pen pressed so hard it has torn the paper in places.

But the content is clear. Devastatingly clear.

choices.text: Take it and press on — no time

t_44.text: You read. The sanctum is quiet, the coral-light steady, and for a few minutes you are alone with the mind of a man who walked to the edge of human knowledge and then kept walking.

test_luck.lucky_text: The temple is silent around you. No footsteps, no interruption. Just you and the words, and the terrible slow understanding they bring.
test_luck.unlucky_text: A sound behind you. Wet footsteps on stone. You turn — a drowned cultist stands in the sanctum doorway, water streaming from its robes, its blank white eyes fixed on you. It has been patrolling. It has found you. The journal will have to wait.

t_44b.text:
The cultist lurches forward. In the close confines of the sanctum, there is nowhere to retreat. Bookshelves at your back, the desk at your hip, and the dead-eyed thing between you and the door.

Fight. Quickly. Before more come.

t_46.text:
The journal tells the story of a man named Elias Morrow. Maritime historian. University of Caravos, Department of Coastal Antiquities. Tenured, respected, boring. The kind of scholar who writes papers that six people read and three of them disagree with.

He came to Saltmere to study the drowned settlement. Routine fieldwork. But in the ruins beneath the Reaches, he found the temple, and in the temple, he found inscriptions that no one had read in four hundred years. They described a being — ancient, vast, bound to the coastal waters by a pact older than the settlement itself. Not a god. Not a demon. Something else. A guardian. The Leviathan was created — or called, or woken — to protect the coast. The old settlement thrived under its watchful presence.

The pact was broken by a rival cult that inverted the binding rituals. The guardian became a predator. The settlement drowned. The Leviathan, unable to fulfil its original purpose, sank into the deep and slept.

Morrow found the inversion. He believed he could reverse it — restore the original pact, turn the predator back into a guardian. His notes are meticulous. His logic is sound. His methods are catastrophically wrong.

The final entry, written in that terrible cramped hand: 'The ritual requires a sacrifice of will, not flesh. I understand this now. The old priests gave their devotion. I have given my sanity. The difference may not matter. The tide comes. I cannot stop what I have started, and I am no longer certain I want to.'

You close the journal. Your hands are steady but something in your chest is not.

t_48.text:
The chest is locked, but the lock is simple — a sailor's footlocker mechanism, not temple work. You force it with your blade. Inside: a cracked stone disc the size of your palm, carved with an inverted spiral. The same mark you have seen throughout this place, throughout your journey. One half of something that was once whole.

Beneath the disc, a rough charcoal map of the temple's rear passages, drawn in Morrow's steady early handwriting. An escape route, perhaps. Or the way to something he wanted to find but never did.

You take both.

t_50.text:
You step back into the cavern's blue-green twilight. The chanting has resumed — thinner now, less certain, but resuming nonetheless. The ritual continues. Morrow is still at the altar, arms raised, voice weaving the threads back together. Whatever you do, you must do it soon.

The temple presses against you from every direction. Stone and water and old, old darkness. The air tastes of decisions that cannot be unmade.

choices.text: Head for the inscription chamber — the symbol calls to you
Investigate a small side-chamber with a plinth

t_52.text:
The inscription chamber is a side-room off the main cavern, easy to miss if you did not know to look for it. The entrance is low — you must duck to enter — and the room beyond is circular, no larger than a ship's cabin. Every surface is carved.

The inscriptions cover the walls from floor to ceiling in concentric spirals, the text growing smaller as it approaches the centre of each wall. The language is not one you recognise, but the symbols are familiar — the inverted spiral, the wave-patterns, the same iconography you have seen throughout the temple. This room is older than the rest. You can feel it. The stone is different, the carving deeper, the coral-light dimmer, as though even the bioluminescence respects the age of this place.

At the centre of the far wall, larger than the rest: a single inscription, bordered by wave-patterns that seem to move in the shifting light.

choices.text: Study the central inscription — you recognise the spiral
Cross-reference with what you read in the journal
Examine the crude drawings on the opposite wall
The writing means nothing to you — leave

t_52b.text:
The inscription resolves.

Not all at once — it comes in pieces, like a coastline emerging from fog. The symbol you recognise is the key. Once you have it, the surrounding text begins to make a kind of sense — not translation, exactly, but comprehension. The meaning beneath the words.

The old pact. Written here, in stone, in a language that predates the settlement by centuries. Not a contract. Not a prayer. Something between the two — a mutual agreement between the coast and the deep, between the people who lived on the shore and the thing that lived beneath it. The Leviathan was not summoned. It was invited. And in return for its protection, the coastal people gave it something: their attention. Their devotion. Not worship — recognition. The acknowledgment that the sea is alive and the things within it deserve to be seen.

The pact was simple. See me, and I will keep you safe.

The inversion was equally simple. Forget me, and I will make you remember.

You stand in the inscription chamber with your hand on the wall and the words of the old pact burning in your mind. This is what Morrow found. This is what he tried to restore with blood and chanting and the wrong kind of devotion. The ritual is not an awakening — it is a distortion of something that was once an act of love.

The Leviathan can be spoken to. Not commanded. Not bound. Spoken to. The old words are right here, carved in stone. All someone has to do is say them and mean them.

t_55.text:
You freeze.

One of the drowned cultists — the one nearest the pillar, swaying with the same tidal rhythm as the rest — is wearing a coat you recognise. Dark blue wool. Brass buttons stamped with the Windborne's crest. The face beneath the hood is grey and bloated, barnacled at the jaw, but the shape of it is familiar in a way that makes your stomach clench.

You sailed with this person. You cannot remember their name, and that failure feels like a wound. But you know the way they stood — slightly favouring the left leg, a fall from the rigging two years ago that never healed right. Even drowned, even hollowed out and filled with the Leviathan's cold purpose, the body remembers its old injuries.

choices.text: Try to reach them — break through the Leviathan's hold
They are gone — fight what remains

t_57.text:
You move closer. The drowned crewmate's white eyes track your approach with the blank patience of the deep. You speak their name — it comes to you suddenly, pulled from some depth of memory you did not know you had. You say it twice, the second time louder.

The swaying stops.

For three seconds — you count them, because counting is the only thing keeping you upright — the white eyes clear. Not fully. Not back to the warm hazel you remember. But enough. The mouth works. Saltwater spills over grey lips and a voice comes out, barely human, compressed by the weight of whatever holds this body upright.

'The altar stone... channels his power. Break it... and he is... exposed. The stone is the...' The voice grinds to a halt. The eyes dim. The swaying resumes.

But you heard enough. The altar is not decoration. It is infrastructure. Destroy it and Morrow loses his connection to whatever force the ritual is gathering. You file this away in the part of your mind that is still capable of tactical thought.

The rest of your mind is occupied with the sound of a name you spoke into a dark temple and the three seconds of recognition you received in return. It will have to be enough. It is more than the sea usually gives back.

choices.text: Move on — honour them by finishing this

t_59.text:
There is a particular cruelty in fighting someone whose name you cannot quite remember. The drowned crewmate lunges with the jerking, puppet-strung motion of all the Leviathan's servants, and you raise your weapon against a face that ate dinner at the same table as you for three years.

The grief is physical. It settles in your chest like ballast stone.

t_60.text:
The corridor narrows as you approach the altar chamber. The walls are closer here, the coral-light brighter, the chanting louder — each step takes you deeper into the machine of the ritual. The stone beneath your feet is wet and the water is rising, slowly but perceptibly. Ankle-deep now. Warmer than it should be.

Ahead, the corridor opens into the altar chamber proper. The green fire is visible from here, casting writhing shadows on the ceiling. The chanting has the cadence of something approaching completion — faster now, higher, building toward a crescendo that makes the stone vibrate beneath your feet.

choices.text: One of the drowned cultists looks familiar
A figure in the shadows — a Windborne crewmate
A hand grabs your ankle from the shadows
Footsteps behind you — a cultist patrol
A passage leads toward the bone pit
Check a flooded sub-corridor for supplies
A dead cultist lies by the wall, clutching something
A natural chimney — you can smell the surface

t_61.text:
You nearly take the hand off at the wrist before you realise it is warm. Living warm. Human warm.

The face that emerges from the shadow of a collapsed pillar is gaunt and terrified and very much alive. A sailor — you can tell by the hands, the rope-calluses, the particular way they squint in dim light. Not Windborne crew, but some other ship's complement, dragged down here by the cult and left to rot in the dark.

'Please,' they whisper. 'Food. Anything. I'll tell you — I know the way. The rear passage. Behind the altar. Morrow's private route. Please.'

The desperation is absolute. This is a person at the end of everything, offering the only thing they have left — information — in exchange for the only thing that matters.

t_62.text:
The corridor opens into the altar chamber. Between you and the altar, three drowned cultists stand in a loose line across the passage — not guarding it, exactly, but occupying it with the passive obstruction of the deeply enthralled. Their white eyes stare at nothing. Their mouths move in silent repetition of the chant.

Behind them, the altar burns. The green fire is taller now, nearly reaching the ceiling, and within it you can see shapes — not solid, not hallucination, but something between. The shadow of something vast moving beneath deep water.

choices.text: Hook across the gap above them
Swing across on the kelp rope
Fire into the group to scatter them

t_63.text:
The first cultist turns at the sound of your approach. The white eyes focus — or rather, the thing behind the white eyes focuses, the Leviathan's attention narrowing through this puppet to the threat that has entered its temple. The drowned mouth opens in a hiss of salt spray.

One at a time. You can do this. One at a time.

t_63b.text:
The first cultist falls. The second turns immediately — no hesitation, no grief for its fallen companion. The Leviathan does not mourn. The Leviathan replaces. The second cultist comes at you with the same jerking, inhuman speed.

Your arms are heavy. The temple's cold is in your bones now.

t_63c.text:
Two down. The third cultist does not wait — it lunges before the second has finished falling, stepping over the body with the indifference of water flowing around a stone. Your weapon is slick with brine. Your breath comes in ragged bursts.

Last one. Finish it.

t_64.text:
You raise the flintlock and fire into the group. The sound is catastrophic in the enclosed space — the shot tears through the nearest cultist's chest and the body simply comes apart, saltwater and grey flesh spraying across the stone. The other two stagger, the chant breaking in their throats. Their white eyes blink — once, twice — with the confused, hurt expression of sleepers jolted awake.

The moment passes. They will recover. But the gap in the line is enough. You run.

t_65.text:
Past the cultists. The altar chamber opens before you in its full terrible scope. The green fire roars. The altar stone pulses with a light that seems to come from within — as though something is trapped inside it, or passing through it, the stone serving as a window between this place and somewhere infinitely deeper.

Morrow stands at the altar's edge, his arms raised, his voice climbing toward a pitch that makes the bones of your skull vibrate. He is close now. Close enough to see the veins standing out on his temples. Close enough to see that his eyes are no longer entirely green — there are flecks of white in them, like sea-foam, like the eyes of the drowned.

choices.text: Go for the altar — destroy it

t_67.text:
You lunge for the altar. The stone is within arm's reach — you can feel the heat of the green fire, can see the spirals carved into the surface turning, turning, as though the altar itself is a mechanism winding toward some final, irreversible conclusion.

Morrow sees you. His voice cuts off mid-syllable. His hand rises, trailing green fire.

test_luck.lucky_text:
You are faster. Your blade comes down on the altar stone with every ounce of strength left in your body. The impact runs up your arm like a lightning strike. The stone cracks — a fissure opens across its surface, bisecting the carved spiral, and the green fire gutters as though a hand has closed around its throat.

Then the stone shatters.

The sound is not a crack but a chord — a deep, resonant note that shakes the cavern floor and sends stalactites crashing from the ceiling into the black water below. The green fire goes out. The cultists collapse as one, their chanting silenced, their bodies crumpling to the ground like puppets with cut strings.

Morrow staggers. His hands clutch at empty air where the fire was. The green drains from his eyes like tide going out, and for one awful moment you see the man beneath — exhausted, terrified, completely aware of what he has done.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
Morrow's hand closes on your wrist. The grip is iron — no, stronger than iron. The cold that flows from his touch is the cold of the deep ocean, the cold of water that has never known sunlight. Your blade strikes the altar but the blow is deflected, skidding across the surface. A crack opens in the stone — damage, but not destruction.

Morrow hurls you backward. You hit the ground hard, the impact driving the air from your lungs. The altar is cracked but still standing. The green fire still burns. But something has changed — the fire is dimmer, the chanting is faltering, and Morrow's face is drawn with a pain that is not physical.

You have hurt it. You have not killed it. And now Morrow is looking at you with the focused attention of a man who will not make the same mistake twice.

t_68.text:
The altar is rubble. Green fire gutters in the cracks, dying. The air in the cavern shifts — the oppressive weight of the ritual lifting like fog burning off under morning sun. You can breathe. For the first time since you entered this temple, you can draw a full breath without the taste of something wrong in it.

The cultists lie in heaps across the cavern floor. Some are breathing — human breathing, the shallow, desperate breaths of people who have been held underwater and suddenly released. Others are still. You do not check which is which. There is no time.

Morrow has not fallen. He stands in the wreckage of his altar, swaying, his hands opening and closing at his sides. The green is gone from his eyes. What remains is worse — the naked, human awareness of a man who has been conscious of everything he has done and unable to stop any of it.

'The stone,' he says. His voice is his own now — quiet, academic, precise. The voice of a university lecturer addressing a seminar. 'You broke the conduit. Good. That was...' He trails off. His hands are shaking. 'That was the right thing to do.'

choices.text: Close on Morrow while he is staggered

t_70.text:
Morrow faces you across the ruined chamber. The green fire — what remains of it — clings to his fingers like phosphorescence on a drowning man's hands. His face is a battlefield. The scholar and the priest and the thing the Leviathan has made of both, all fighting for the same set of features.

'You do not understand what you are interfering with,' he says, and his voice carries the harmonics of the deep — human words underscored by something vast and cold. 'This was never about destruction. This was about restoration. The pact. The old covenant. I was trying to bring it back.'

He raises his hands. The green fire intensifies. Whatever lucidity the altar's destruction gave him is fading, swallowed by the Leviathan's hold.

'But you are here now. And the tide will not wait for explanations.'

choices.text: You found the journal, Morrow. I read every word.
Fight him — weakened as he is

t_70b.text:
The words hit him harder than your blade could. You see it — the flinch, the way his hands drop half an inch, the green fire stuttering in his palms. For three heartbeats, Elias Morrow — the real Elias Morrow, the maritime historian from the University of Caravos — surfaces.

'You read it,' he whispers. 'All of it?'

'All of it. The settlement. The guardian. The inversion. You were trying to fix something that was broken four hundred years ago, and you broke yourself instead.'

His jaw tightens. Something moves behind his eyes — not the green fire but something older, something that remembers lecture halls and footnotes and the particular joy of solving a puzzle no one else could solve. 'The methodology was sound,' he says, and the academic precision of the statement in this place, at this moment, surrounded by the wreckage of everything he has built, is the saddest thing you have ever heard.

'But the execution,' he continues, and his voice breaks, 'the execution was catastrophic.'

The moment holds. Then the green fire surges back. The Leviathan reclaims him. The hands come up, wreathed in cold flame, and the man disappears behind the priest.

'Still,' he says, and the harmonics are back, the deep-water resonance that makes your bones ache. 'We are past the point of conversation.'

t_72.text:
Morrow comes at you with the full force of the Leviathan behind him. The green fire coils around his arms like living things — serpentine, hungry, burning cold instead of hot. His movements are no longer human. The Leviathan has abandoned subtlety. It moves him like a current moves a ship — with total authority and no regard for the hull.

The fight begins. It is the hardest of your life.

Not because of the fire, or the cold, or the strength the deep has given him. Because every time your blade connects, you see his face — the face of a man who wanted to fix something beautiful and ancient and broke himself trying. Every time he strikes, you hear the academic precision in his voice, giving way to something that is not a voice at all.

This is not a monster. This is a mistake. A catastrophic, irreversible, tragically human mistake.

You fight it anyway.

t_74.text:
Morrow falls.

Not dramatically — not the theatrical collapse of a villain in a story. He simply stops. The green fire gutters out of his hands like water running from a cupped palm. His knees buckle. He sits down on the wet stone floor with the careful deliberation of a man who has been standing too long and has finally given himself permission to rest.

The chanting has stopped. The cavern is silent except for the sound of water — always water, always the tide, always the sea asserting its claim on everything above and below.

choices.text: Listen to his last words — you know the old pact
Kneel beside him — you read his journal

t_74a.text:
You kneel beside him. Up close, the green is gone from his eyes entirely. What remains is grey — the grey of deep water, the grey of exhaustion, the grey of a man who has been carrying something too heavy for too long.

'You found the old words,' he says. His voice is barely audible. The academic precision is gone. What remains is simpler — a man speaking plainly at the end. 'The inscription chamber. You understood it.'

'The pact,' you say. 'See me, and I will keep you safe.'

His eyes close. Something that might be a smile crosses his face — the ghost of the expression a scholar wears when a student finally grasps the point. 'Yes. That simple. That impossibly, heartbreakingly simple.' He coughs. Saltwater on his lips. 'I made it so complicated. Rituals and sacrifice and the mathematics of devotion. And the answer was always just... see me.'

He reaches into his robe and pulls out something — a fragment of stone, carved with half a spiral. The other half of the seal. 'Take it. The binding chamber is below. Where the water meets the stone. Speak the words there. Not to command. Not to bind. Just... to see."

His hand drops. The fragment rolls across the wet stone toward you.

t_74b.text:
You kneel beside him. The green is fading from his eyes like tide going out, leaving behind grey — human grey, tired grey, the colour of a man who has not slept in a very long time.

'The journal,' he says. He does not ask. He can see it in your face. 'You read it. Good. Then you know I was not always...' He gestures weakly at the cavern, the altar wreckage, the collapsed cultists. 'This.'

'I know you were a historian,' you say. 'A good one. You found something remarkable and it consumed you.'

'Consumed.' He tastes the word. 'Yes. That is accurate. The Leviathan does not possess — it is not that crude. It offers you the depths of your own obsession and waits for you to drown in them. I drowned willingly. I thought I was swimming.'

He coughs. More saltwater. His breathing is shallow, each inhale costing more than the last. 'The binding chamber. Below the cavern, where the water meets the stone. That is where the original pact was sealed. If someone could speak the old words there — truly speak them, with understanding, not ritual...' He trails off. His eyes are dimming.

'I could not do it. The Leviathan's hold made me incapable of the one thing the pact requires. Sincerity. You cannot speak with genuine devotion when you are already devoured.'

His hand finds yours. The grip is weak, human, warm. 'Perhaps you can.'

t_74c.text:
You stand over him. The green drains from his eyes. What is left is the face of a stranger — gaunt, hollowed, the face of a man who has been eaten from the inside by something he invited in.

'Too late,' he says. His voice is flat, drained of the resonance that filled the cavern moments ago. Without the Leviathan behind it, he is just a man with a ruined throat and nothing left to say. 'It does not matter what you do to me. The ritual is past the point of interruption. The tide comes. The Leviathan wakes. Nothing stops that now.'

He coughs. Saltwater. His eyes fix on the ceiling — on the darkness above where the coral-light does not reach. 'I thought I could fix it,' he whispers, and for a moment the academic is back, the man who spent years in archives and libraries and thought understanding was the same as control. 'I thought if I could just... understand the mechanism... I could reverse the damage. Four hundred years of damage. Undone by one man with a good theory.'

The laugh that comes out of him is the worst sound you have ever heard in this temple. Worse than the chanting. Worse than the drowned voices. The laugh of a man who finally gets the joke.

'I was wrong about everything except the Leviathan. It is real. It is down there. And now it is awake.'

t_75.text:
Morrow's breathing slows. His eyes are closed. The last of the green fire leaves him in a thin wisp, like steam from a cooling kettle, and drifts toward the ceiling where it dissolves into the darkness.

With his final breath, he speaks. Not in the language of the chant — in something older, something that has the rhythm of waves on a shore and the cadence of a lullaby sung to a sleeping coast. The old words. The original pact. He speaks them clearly, precisely, with the diction of a man who has spent his entire life learning to say exactly what he means.

The words settle into you like stones into deep water. You will not forget them. You could not forget them if you tried. They are in you now, the way the sea is in everything that has ever touched it.

Morrow's hand relaxes. The fragment of the ancient seal lies between you on the wet stone. You pick it up. It is warm.

The binding chamber waits below.

choices.text: Rise. There is still work to be done.

t_76.text:
Morrow does not move again.

The green fire is gone. The cultists lie scattered across the cavern floor, some breathing, some not. The altar — intact or shattered, depending on what you did — stands or lies in the centre of the chamber, and the black water is rising. Perceptibly now. What was ankle-deep is shin-deep. The tide is coming in.

The cavern groans. Deep in the stone, something shifts — not an earthquake but a settling, as though the temple is adjusting itself to a new reality. The coral-light flickers. Dims. Brightens again with a different quality — less blue, more green. The colour of deep water.

And from below — from beneath the cavern floor, from the flooded passages and drowned chambers and the places where the stone gives way to the sea — you feel it. Not a sound. Not a tremor. A presence. Vast and slow and utterly awake.

The Leviathan.

It has been down there all along, beneath the ritual, beneath the chanting, beneath Morrow's desperate mathematics. The ritual may have failed — or been interrupted, or corrupted — but the thing it was trying to reach has noticed. The attention of something the size of an ocean floor turns toward this cavern, toward this temple, toward you, with the patient, overwhelming focus of the tide.

The water is at your knees. Time is a resource you have almost exhausted.

choices.text: Head deeper — toward the binding chamber
A warm glow emanates from a natural grotto nearby
Search Morrow's sanctum while there is still time

t_78.text:
You wade through rising water toward the rear of the cavern. The passage behind the altar — the one Morrow's map showed, the one you spotted from the ledge, the one the dying sailor traded for a meal — opens before you like a throat.

The water is waist-deep and climbing. The coral-light has turned fully green now, the bioluminescence responding to whatever is happening below. Your feet find steps carved into the stone floor — a stairway descending into the flooded dark. You follow it down.

The water reaches your chest. Your neck. You tilt your head back, breathing the last inches of air trapped against the ceiling, and then the stairs level out and the passage opens into a final chamber.

The binding chamber.

It is not large. A circular room, barely twenty feet across, every wall carved with the spiral inscriptions you have learned to read. The water fills it to the ceiling — you are fully submerged now, holding your breath, the green coral-light bright enough to see by.

And then the floor of the chamber moves.

Not the floor. Something beneath the floor. Something vast and dark and alive, shifting in the depths below the carved stone, pressing against the boundary between the temple and the open ocean. The stone groans. Cracks web across the floor. Through the cracks, you see it — not clearly, not entirely, but enough.

An eye. As large as the chamber itself. Green and gold and ancient beyond any measure you possess. It looks at you. Not through you. Not past you. At you. Directly, specifically, with the focused attention of something that has been waiting for someone to come down here and look back.

The eye blinks, slow as a century turning.

The water around you churns. The walls shake. The surface of the chamber floor heaves upward and cracks apart, and through the gap, something begins to rise.

You kick for the surface. The last air pocket is above the stairway, a thin bright line between water and stone. You break through, gasping, and behind you the sound of the binding chamber tearing itself open fills the temple like the voice of a god clearing its throat.

The aspect of the Leviathan is surfacing.

Not the whole creature — you understand instinctively that the whole creature would not fit in this temple, would not fit in this ocean, would not fit in anything smaller than a mythology. What rises through the shattered floor is a fragment, a tendril, an expression of something so vast that even its smallest gesture reshapes the architecture of the world around it.

It fills the passage behind you. Green-gold light pours from it. The water surges. The temple shakes.

Act Three is over. What comes next will be decided in the open water, in the full presence of the thing beneath the Reaches.

Prepare yourself. The tide has turned.

t_80.text:
A side passage branches off the main corridor — narrow, low-ceilinged, the kind of space that would make a claustrophobe weep. The coral-light here is dim, pulsing with a slower rhythm than the main cavern, as though this section of the temple is asleep. Or pretending to be.

The passage opens into a small chamber. Cells. Six of them, carved into the rock, separated by walls of worked stone worn smooth by centuries of tidal wash. Iron bars — corroded green, some broken — seal each cell. Behind the bars: darkness, and the smell of old death, and in one cell, a pile of rags that might have been a person, once.

This is where the temple kept its failures. The ones the Leviathan could not use.

choices.text: Investigate the corridor beyond the cells
Leave — this place has nothing for you

t_82.text:
Most of the cells are empty. Scoured clean by the tides, nothing left but smooth stone and the faintest salt-stain outlines of bodies long dissolved. But the last cell — the one farthest from the passage entrance — holds something.

A skeleton in sailor's clothes, curled against the far wall in the foetal position. The clothes are not ancient — they are modern, or near enough. Canvas trousers. A wool shirt. The kind of kit any merchant sailor would wear. Beside the skeleton, scratched into the stone wall with what must have been a belt buckle, a single word repeated dozens of times, growing smaller and more frantic with each repetition:

REMEMBER REMEMBER REMEMBER REMEMBER

The word fills the wall. It is the most human thing you have seen in this temple.

t_84.text:
You reach through the broken bars. There is nothing of value on the body — the pockets are empty, the belt buckle worn smooth from scratching. But tucked inside the shirt, against the ribcage, is a folded piece of paper sealed in wax.

You crack the seal. The paper is a letter, never sent. The handwriting is neat at the beginning and desperate at the end:

'Mariela — I do not think I am leaving this place. The priests have stopped bringing food and I can hear the chanting again, which means another ritual is coming. I want you to know that I did not choose this. None of us chose this. The sea took us and the priests kept us and the thing beneath the temple watched us with its great slow eye and I think it was sorry. I think it did not want any of this. I think it is as trapped as we are.

I love you. I am sorry I went to sea. I am sorry I cannot come home. Remember me as I was, not as whatever this place makes of what I leave behind.

Yours, always. Tam.'

You fold the letter and put it in your coat. You do not know Mariela. You will probably never find her. But the letter deserves to exist somewhere other than a dead man's shirt in a drowned temple.

The cells have nothing else to offer. You leave.

t_85.text:
A flooded library. The words feel wrong together, but there is no other description. The room is half-submerged — knee-deep water covering the lower shelves, the upper ones still dry, crammed with scrolls and bound volumes in languages you cannot read. The coral-light is brightest here, as though the temple values its books.

Most of the volumes are ruined. Saltwater and centuries have reduced them to pulp and memory. But on the highest shelf, sealed in the same oilskin Morrow used for his journal, a handful survive. You cannot read them, but their existence feels important — evidence that this place was built by people who valued knowledge enough to protect it from the sea.

Behind a collapsed shelf, wedged into an alcove, you find a clay flask. You pull the cork. Rum. Different from the Saltmere dark — older, smoother, with a warmth that has nothing to do with alcohol. Temple rum. Brewed or stored here centuries ago and preserved by the cold.

You take it. In a place like this, warmth is a weapon.

t_87.text:
The passage descends into a natural cavern — not carved, not built, but formed by the slow dissolution of limestone over millennia. The floor is covered in bones.

Not neatly arranged. Not ritually placed. Just... bones. Hundreds of them, possibly thousands, scattered across the cavern floor like driftwood on a beach after a storm. Skulls and femurs and ribcages and the small, delicate bones of hands and feet, all bleached white by the salt water that floods this chamber with every tide.

The bones of drowned sailors. Decades of them. Perhaps centuries. Every ship the Reaches has swallowed, every crew the Leviathan has claimed — their remains have settled here, in this natural depression in the temple floor, washed in by the currents and left behind when the tide retreats.

The scale of it makes your stomach turn. This is not a graveyard. This is an accumulation. The sea does not bury its dead — it deposits them.

choices.text: Search the bones — a crewmate might be among them
Look for familiar faces among the dead
Leave — you cannot help the dead

t_87b.text:
You move through the bones, trying not to look too closely, failing. Your eyes scan the detritus for anything familiar — a coat, a ring, a particular shape of jaw or brow. You killed your first mate on the Windborne. You took the compass and the key from the body. But there were others. A full crew. Twenty-three souls who went down with the ship.

You find one.

Not a body — the sea has taken that. But a ring, still circling a finger bone, silver with a flat signet. You know this ring. Your bosun wore it. She said it was her grandmother's, said it was the only thing she owned that was not issued by the navy or bought in a port.

You take the ring and slip it onto your smallest finger, where it fits perfectly. You will bring it back to the surface. The sea cannot have everything.

t_87c.text:
You search the bones with the desperate, methodical attention of someone who does not want to find what they are looking for. Your first mate is still on the Windborne — drowned but standing, held upright by the Leviathan's will. You spared them. You walked away. That was a choice, and you stand by it.

But the rest of the crew — the ones who went down with the ship, the ones who did not surface as the Leviathan's puppets — they are somewhere. Some of them may be here.

You find a boot. Standard navy issue, the kind every sailor on the Windborne wore. The leather is salt-crusted but intact. Inside, scratched into the sole with a knife-point in the way sailors mark their gear, a set of initials you recognise. Your cook. The one who made terrible coffee and sang worse songs and kept everyone's spirits above the waterline when the weather turned.

You hold the boot for a long time. Then you set it down carefully, upright, facing the passage out. Pointing toward the sea. Toward home.

It is a small gesture. It changes nothing. You do it anyway.

t_89.text: A door in the corridor wall — heavy, iron-bound, unmarked. It could be anything. Armoury, store-room, trap. The hinges are oiled, which means someone has used it recently. You push it open.

test_luck.lucky_text: Behind the door: a small alcove, dry, with a wooden shelf holding supplies — provisions wrapped in oilskin, still fresh. Someone's emergency cache, perhaps Morrow's own. You take what you can carry.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
The door swings open onto a pressure plate. You hear the mechanism — a click, a hiss of compressed air — but you are already committed, your weight on the wrong foot. A jet of salt water blasts from a nozzle concealed in the wall, hitting you square in the chest and slamming you against the opposite wall. The pressure is enormous — a trap designed to kill or incapacitate, and it nearly does both.

You pick yourself up, bruised and soaked. The trap resets with a mechanical patience that suggests it has been doing this for centuries.

t_89b.text:
The provisions are basic — ship's biscuit and salted fish, the universal diet of anyone who works on or near the sea. Not fresh, but edible. You wrap them in your coat and move on.

The alcove holds nothing else of value. The door closes behind you with a click that sounds final.

t_90.text:
Footsteps behind you. Wet, heavy, rhythmic — the gait of something that moves by compulsion rather than choice. You turn.

Two drowned cultists block the passage you just came through. Their white eyes fix on you simultaneously, with the synchronised attention of a single mind operating two bodies. The Leviathan has noticed the disruption in its temple. It is sending its agents to investigate.

The first cultist lurches forward. The second follows half a step behind.

t_90b.text: The cultist comes at you with the jerking, graceless violence of a marionette in a gale. Behind it, the second waits — patient, unhurried, its turn will come. The Leviathan is efficient with its resources.

t_91.text:
The first cultist collapses. The second steps over the body without acknowledgment — no grief, no hesitation, just the mechanical advance of something that has forgotten what it means to care about the fallen.

It is on you before the first has finished falling.

t_80b.text:
A flooded sub-corridor branches from the main passage. The water here is deeper — chest-high and murky, visibility reduced to arm's length. Something moves in the clouded water ahead. A grey shape, sleek and fast, patrolling the submerged corridor with the proprietary confidence of a predator in its own territory.

A reef shark. Drawn into the temple by the same tidal currents that flood these passages twice a day. It circles, watching you with flat black eyes that hold no malice — just the patient appraisal of something deciding whether you are food.

choices.text: Fight the shark and cross the corridor

t_80c.text:
The shark comes at you in a rush — a grey torpedo of muscle and teeth, cutting through the water with a speed that makes your land-trained reflexes feel hopelessly outmatched. Fighting underwater is a lesson in humility. Every swing is slow. Every dodge is slower. The shark does not share these limitations.

You brace yourself against the corridor wall and bring your weapon to bear.

t_80d.text:
The shark drifts away, trailing a cloud of dark blood. It will not die — reef sharks are tougher than theology — but it has decided you are not worth the effort. It retreats into the murk, and the corridor is yours.

On the far side, wedged into a crack in the wall: a length of rope braided from kelp. Enchanted kelp, by the feel of it — the fibres are unnaturally strong, and the rope does not have the slimy texture you would expect from something grown in saltwater. Useful. You coil it and sling it over your shoulder.

t_10b.text:
From the ledge, you spot another opening in the cavern wall — lower than your current position, partially hidden by a curtain of hanging coral. It leads away from the ritual chamber, deeper into the temple's auxiliary spaces. The passages beyond look older, less maintained. The coral-light is thinner there, the stone more worn.

A side route. Away from the chanting, away from the altar. Into the temple's forgotten quarters.

choices.text: Follow the amber glow down a branching corridor
Return to the main cavern ledge

t_2.text:
The armoury door is locked. The iron resists your shoulder, your blade, your boot. Whoever built this lock built it for centuries, and centuries have not diminished it. Without the right key, this door is a wall with pretensions.

The chanting from the right fork grows louder. The temple does not care about your frustration. The temple has other concerns.

t_10c.text:
The bone pit lies down a natural passage to the west of the main cavern. The smell reaches you before the sight — salt and age and the particular flat odour of bones that have been washed clean by decades of tidal water. If you want answers about what this place has done to people, this is where you will find them.

But answers have a cost. The temple charges admission in nightmares.

choices.text: Leave it — some things are better not seen

t_85b.text:
A passage branches toward the flooded library. The entrance is low and half-submerged — you will need to wade, then possibly swim. The coral-light is brightest in this direction, which suggests the room beyond has significance the temple wants to preserve.

Or it could be a dead end. In this place, significance and dead ends are often the same thing.

t_10d.text:
From the ledge, the full scope of the temple's layout is visible. The main cavern dominates — the altar, the cultists, the green fire. But branching from it like veins from a heart, you can see side passages leading to the temple's auxiliary chambers.

To the east: the colonnade, and beyond it, the approach to the altar. To the west: the cells, the bone pit, the flooded library. Below and behind the altar: the rear passage toward whatever lies deeper.

The chanting fills the space like water fills a cup — completely, leaving no room for anything else.

choices.text: Head to the east colonnade — toward the altar
Drop directly to the cavern floor

t_r1.text:
You return to the main cavern. The chanting continues. The green fire still burns. Whatever you have done in the temple's side chambers, the ritual has not paused to accommodate your curiosity.

The altar awaits. Morrow awaits. The tide awaits. None of them are patient.

choices.text: A drowned cultist kneels in the corridor ahead
You hear singing — a shanty, from behind the wall

t_21.text:
You push through the door into what was once a meditation chamber — a small, circular room with a domed ceiling carved to resemble the underside of a wave. The walls are lined with alcoves, each holding a clay oil lamp long since guttered. In the centre, a stone bench worn smooth by the bodies of those who sat here.

The room is dry. It is the only dry room you have found in this temple, and the absence of water feels almost sacred — a pocket of air preserved through centuries of flooding by some careful engineering of the doorway and drainage channels. Someone wanted this place to survive the tides.

On the bench, scratched into the stone by a hand that had no tools: the inverted spiral. And beneath it, in the same crude lettering: THE SEA REMEMBERS WHAT WE FORGET.

A cultist wrote this. Or a prisoner. Or a priest from the original temple, four centuries dead. There is no way to tell. The words exist. That is all that matters.

t_33.text:
The colonnade continues past the wraith's territory into a section of the temple that feels different — older, the stonework rougher, the coral-light taking on a warmer hue. An alcove opens to your left, screened by a curtain of living kelp.

Behind the kelp: a niche in the wall, and in the niche, a small stone figure. Not a statue — too crude for that. A carving, rough and ancient, depicting something that might be a whale or a serpent or a wave or all three at once. The figure is warm to the touch, despite the temple's chill.

The original temple-builders' conception of the Leviathan. Not the predator Morrow's ritual is trying to wake. The guardian. The protector. The thing that watched the coast and kept it safe.

It looks almost gentle.

t_33b.text:
You place your hand on the stone figure. The warmth intensifies — not heat, but a sensation of presence. Of being noticed. Of something vast and old and not unkind turning a fraction of its attention toward you and finding you... acceptable.

The sensation fades. The carving is just stone again. But something lingers — a sense of connection, thin as a thread, stretched between you and whatever sleeps in the deep places beneath the temple.

You feel steadier. The cold touches you less. The chanting seems quieter, as though the sound has been turned down by a fraction.

t_36.text:
The passage behind the sanctum door leads to a corridor lined with alcoves — supply caches, by the look of them, stocked with the necessities of a long subterranean occupation. Most are empty or ruined by flooding. But one alcove, higher than the waterline, still holds its contents: clay jars sealed with wax, containing what you hope is drinkable water or something stronger.

You crack a seal. Rum. The Drowned Priests apparently maintained their vices along with their theology.

t_65c.text:
The altar chamber is behind you now. Ahead, the passage narrows and descends. The water rises with each step — shin-deep, knee-deep, thigh-deep. The coral-light takes on a greenish cast that has nothing to do with the bioluminescence and everything to do with what waits below.

Behind you: the sound of the ritual collapsing, cultists moaning, stone settling. Ahead: silence, and the slow pressure of something waiting.

There is no going back from this point. The temple knows it. The tide knows it. The thing in the deep knows it.

You know it too.

t_r2.text:
The corridors of the temple blur together — stone and water and coral-light and the distant sound of chanting that never quite stops. You have been in this place too long. The cold is in your bones. The dark is in your eyes. The salt taste of the air has become the default flavour of your world.

But you are still moving. Still thinking. Still alive. In this place, that is a victory in itself.

choices.text: Press on toward the altar chamber
Search the side passages for supplies
Examine the writing scratched on the corridor wall

t_r3.text:
You climb back to the high ledge overlooking the cavern. From here, the ritual is a miniature — the cultists small as chess pieces, the green fire a candle flame, Morrow a dark figure at the centre of his congregation.

The perspective helps. Sometimes you need to see the whole board before you can make a move.

choices.text: Drop to the cavern floor and charge

t_r4.text:
Back at the colonnade. The pillars provide cover but the wraith's territory is behind you now. Ahead, the antechamber opens onto the ritual space. The chanting fills the air like water filling a lung — pervasive, inescapable, pressing on the inside of your skull.

The altar is forty feet away. Morrow is at its centre. The decision is the same one it has always been: how to reach him, and what to do when you get there.

t_r5.text:
You wade back through the rising water. The temple is changing around you — passages that were dry an hour ago are ankle-deep now. The tide is coming in and it will not stop until everything below the waterline is submerged.

The main corridor is still passable. You have time. Not much. But enough, if you do not waste it.

t_92.text:
A coral garden. There is no other word for it. The side-chamber opens into a space so thick with bioluminescent growth that the air itself seems to glow — not the cold blue-green of the corridors but something warmer, almost amber, the colour of late afternoon sunlight filtered through honey.

The coral here is not the encrusting variety that covers the temple walls. These are branching formations, delicate as antlers, growing from every surface in patterns that seem deliberately cultivated. Someone tended this garden. Someone cared enough about beauty to maintain it in a place that time and tide have stripped of everything else.

In the centre of the garden, a small pool of still water reflects the amber light. The reflection is not quite accurate — it shows the coral as it might have been centuries ago, the branches longer, the colours deeper, the garden in its full glory. A window into the temple's past. Or a memory the water refuses to release.

You could stay here for hours. You cannot afford minutes.

t_93.text:
The passage ahead is not empty. Kneeling in the corridor, its robes spread in the shallow water like the mantle of a jellyfish, is a drowned cultist. But this one is different. It is not swaying. It is not chanting. It is still — perfectly, utterly still — with its forehead pressed to the stone floor and its arms outstretched in a posture of supplication that has nothing to do with the Leviathan's ritual.

This one is praying. Not to the Leviathan. To whatever came before. The posture is older than the cult — you have seen it in the inscriptions, the way the original temple-builders addressed their guardian. Forehead to stone. Arms wide. See me.

The cultist does not move as you approach. Does not move as you pass. The white eyes are open, staring at the floor, and something in them is not the blank hunger of the Leviathan's servants but something quieter. Sadder. The last ember of whoever this person was before the sea took them, burning in a place the Leviathan cannot reach.

You step over the outstretched arms and continue. Some prayers are not meant to be interrupted.

t_94.text:
A narrow fissure in the corridor wall opens onto a natural chimney — a vertical shaft in the rock, roughly cylindrical, stretching upward into darkness. The walls are slick with condensation and the faintest current of air moves through the shaft, carrying with it the smell of the surface. Salt and wind and the memory of open sky.

The chimney is too narrow to climb, but the draught is real. Somewhere far above, this shaft opens onto the outcrop. A connection to the world outside. Proof that the surface still exists, that the sky is still there, that this temple has not swallowed the entire world.

You stand beneath the chimney and breathe. Just breathe. The air from above is the sweetest thing you have tasted since you descended the stairs. After a minute, you move on. But you carry the taste of sky with you.

It helps more than it should.

t_95.text:
The water here is deeper than elsewhere — waist-high and thick with suspended sediment that reduces visibility to nothing. You are wading blind. Your feet find the passage floor by feel, each step testing the stone before committing.

Something brushes your leg. Not the current. Something with intention. You freeze.

It passes. A shape in the murk, glimpsed and gone — longer than your arm, sinuous, trailing phosphorescence. Not a shark. Something else. Something that lives in these flooded passages the way rats live in a ship's hold — native, territorial, tolerated.

The touch was exploratory, not aggressive. A question, not an attack. You are being assessed by something that has decided, for now, that you are not interesting enough to eat.

You wade faster. The water shallows. Whatever lives in the deep passages remains behind, a presence felt but never fully seen. Some knowledge is better left incomplete.

t_96.text:
You find them in an alcove behind a collapsed pillar — a pair of boots, set neatly side by side against the wall. Sailor's boots. The leather is cracked but the stitching is intact, and someone has cleaned them. Recently. The salt-crust has been scraped away, the laces re-tied in neat bows.

A drowned cultist's boots. But cleaned by human hands — by whatever scrap of personhood survives beneath the Leviathan's hold. Someone in this temple, someone already dead, someone already taken, still cares enough about their boots to clean them. Still remembers what it means to maintain your kit, to take pride in small things, to be a sailor.

The boots are too small for you. You leave them where they are, neatly paired, facing the passage out. Another small gesture. Another thing the sea cannot have.

t_97.text:
A dead cultist. Not drowned-dead — properly dead, the body weeks old, curled in a foetal position at the base of the corridor wall. The cause of death is not immediately apparent. No wounds. No signs of violence. The expression on the grey, desiccated face is peaceful — almost serene.

The body is clutching something. You pry open the stiff fingers. A piece of coral, broken from the wall, shaped by patient hands into a rough approximation of a bird. A seagull, perhaps. Or an albatross. The kind of thing a sailor would carve to pass the time on long watches.

The Leviathan's hold broke. Somehow, for some reason, this person was released — and the first thing they did was carve a bird. The last act of a mind that remembered what freedom looked like.

You pocket the carving. It weighs almost nothing.

t_98.text:
The corridor branches. Left: the faint amber glow of something warm. Right: the sound of running water, which in this place means either a natural spring or the tide finding a new way in.

Both routes rejoin the main passage ahead. The question is not where they go but what you will find along the way.

t_99.text:
The wall here is different. The inscriptions that cover most of the temple — the spirals, the wave-patterns, the dense text in the old language — give way to something simpler. Pictures. Crude, almost childlike drawings scratched into the stone with a sharp tool.

The first panel shows a coastline. Houses. People on the shore, arms raised. In the water, a shape — vast, curving, suggested rather than drawn. A whale. A serpent. A wave with eyes.

The second panel shows the same coastline, the same people. But now the figure in the water is smaller, and the people's arms are lowered. They have turned away. They have stopped looking.

The third panel shows the water rising. The houses submerged. The people gone. And the figure in the water, alone, its great suggested eye staring at an empty shore.

The fourth panel is blank. Either the artist ran out of wall, or ran out of story, or could not bring themselves to draw what came next.

You stand before the blank panel for a long time. Then you press your hand against it. A gesture that means nothing. A gesture that means everything.

t_100.text:
You are getting tired. The temple has been measuring your strength in small increments — a point of stamina here, a nerve frayed there, a certainty eroded. The cumulative weight of everything you have seen and fought and learned presses on you like the water that rises around your ankles.

But you are still here. You are still moving. You came down these stairs because someone had to, and you are still the someone. That has not changed. That will not change.

The chanting ahead is louder. The green fire brighter. The water warmer. Whatever is coming, it is coming soon.

You tighten your grip on your weapon. You check your footing. You breathe.

You move forward.

t_101.text:
A side chamber. Small, circular, the stone worn smooth as a riverbed. In the centre, a plinth — not an altar, something older and simpler. A flat stone worn concave by centuries of something resting on it. The object is gone now. All that remains is the impression it left.

The plinth is carved with a single word in the old language. You cannot read it, but the shape of the letters suggests something reverential. Whatever sat here was valued. Whatever sat here was the centre of this room's purpose.

You think of the seal fragments — the cracked stone discs carved with the inverted spiral. This plinth was made for a complete seal. The two halves, joined, would fit the concavity perfectly.

The binding chamber must have a plinth like this one. Or perhaps this is the original, and the binding chamber is the copy. In a temple this old, it is difficult to tell which came first — the ritual or the room.

t_102.text:
You hear singing.

Not the chanting — that is constant, that is the temple's heartbeat, you have learned to filter it the way a sailor filters the sound of waves. This is different. A single voice, thin and wavering, carrying a melody that tugs at something in your chest you thought the sea had numbed.

A shanty. A working song, the kind sailors sing to coordinate the hauling of ropes and the turning of capstans. You know this one. You have sung this one, on the Windborne, in better weather, in a better life.

The voice comes from behind a collapsed section of wall. You cannot see the singer. You do not need to. A drowned cultist, trapped beneath fallen stone, singing a song it learned in a life it can no longer remember. The melody is perfect. The words are wrong — garbled, drowned, the consonants softened by a throat full of saltwater. But the tune is right. The tune survives everything.

You stand in the corridor and listen until the song ends. It does not end. It begins again, from the top, the same melody, the same broken words, on a loop that will continue until the singer is released or the sea reclaims everything.

You cannot help them. You know this. You listen for one more verse anyway.

Then you go.

t_103.text:
The passage widens into a natural grotto — a space not carved but formed by the slow work of water on limestone. Stalactites hang from the ceiling like the teeth of something vast. The floor is covered in a thin film of water that reflects the coral-light so perfectly that you seem to be walking on the ceiling.

In the centre of the grotto, rising from the shallow water like a pillar: a column of natural stone, worn by the tide into a shape that is almost, but not quite, a human figure. A woman, perhaps. Or a wave caught mid-break. The stone is warm.

This is a place of convergence. You can feel it — currents of something that is not water flowing through the rock, meeting here, pooling in the stone figure's outstretched hands. The temple was not built around the Leviathan. The temple was built around this — around the places where the deep speaks and the stone listens.

You are standing in one of those places now. The stone figure regards you with the patience of a thing that has been waiting for four centuries and can wait four more.

The feeling of being seen is overwhelming. Not judged. Not threatened. Seen. The way the original pact described — simple recognition, mutual regard, the acknowledgment of presence.

You nod. It feels like the right thing to do. The stone does not nod back. But the warmth in the air increases, just slightly, and the coral-light brightens, and something in the water at your feet settles into stillness.

Something has been acknowledged. Something old and patient and not unkind.

t_104.text:
A sealed door. Unlike the others in this temple, this one is not iron-bound or barnacle-crusted. It is stone — a single slab, fitted so precisely into the passage wall that you can barely see the seam. There is no lock. There is no handle. There is only a single carved symbol at the centre of the slab: the inverted spiral.

You press your hand against it. The stone is warm. The spiral seems to turn beneath your palm — not physically, but in the way an optical illusion turns, the eye finding motion in stillness.

The door does not open. Whatever it guards remains behind the slab, patient and sealed. The spiral turns and turns and you watch it and understand that some passages are not meant to be walked in this lifetime.

Perhaps in the next. Perhaps never. The temple keeps its own counsel.

t_105.text:
Scratched into the corridor wall, at eye level, in a hand that trembles with either cold or urgency:

IF YOU ARE READING THIS YOU ARE NOT ONE OF THEM
THE MAN AT THE ALTAR WAS NOT ALWAYS LIKE THIS
HE CAME TO STUDY NOT TO WORSHIP
THE SEA CHANGES WHAT IT TOUCHES
GOD HELP HIM
GOD HELP US ALL

The writing ends. Beneath it, in a different hand — steadier, more deliberate — a single addendum:

THERE IS NO GOD HERE
ONLY THE TIDE

You read the words and the words read you back. Then you continue down the corridor, carrying them with you like stones in your pockets.
